item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I mean, he's a real person, and famous enough in russian Internet. C'mon this picture isn't even redacted! You could hide his eyes at least

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Link led directly to image. Source is here, which is under a 3.0 unported license - thus, compliant with the site's license.

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Does Ilia Varlamov, famous russian blogger, knows about using this picture?
C'mon, it was even named "Ilia Varlamov"!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea. A guy who not only has immortality of the "can only die from an injury that either cannot be recovered from unaided if it can be recovered from at all" variety, but who compels people to make sure he’s okay when something happens & got stuck with that ability being impossible to switch off because he died is a new 1 for me. My 1 nitpick:

The range and intensity of SCP-3430's anomalous properties are not impeded by physical obstacles, but only affect sapient subjects; non-human animals and machines are not affected by SCP-3430.

The phrasing is a little ambiguous. It says it only affects sapient subjects. That I can understand. But the statement that "non-human animals & machines are not affected" makes it unclear whether or not sapient things in the following categories are effected:

  • inorganic lifeforms (like artificial intelligences, SCP-085, or SCP-2353)
  • animals of a species other than humans (like SCP-953 & victims of the animal transformation zipper)
  • otherwise-biologically-&-mentally-baseline-human individuals with both anomalous traits &/or abilities & at least the minimum level of sapience that a non-anomalous baseline human would need to have in order to be susceptible1(SCP-239, SCP-134, the Little Misters, SCP-2800, the Maize Angel.)
  • sapient entities that do not fall into the above categories (any sapient thing covered by the "extraterrestrial" tag)

The examples provided are for the convenience of anyone confused by what I mean by those oddly specific categories.

Footnotes
1. I’m not counting cases where an otherwise non-anomalous baseline human-individual's level of/capacity for sapience is negatively impacted by a mundane mental disorder as an anomalous trait here — low-functioning autistic people, anybody? I'm also assuming there’s a level of sapience below which SCP-3430 has no effect, & that there are levels of sapience. Correct me if I’m wrong on any of those assumptions, author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So he's already anomalous, in that people are compelled to comfort him til he's okay.

But the effect lasts after death and is still connected to his corpse, however since he's dead he can never be okay again, so they just cling to him til they die.

Like it.

Love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 because you told me what love was

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks for helping me get this article to 50+ upvotes! It’s a new personal record!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough. I’m at least glad you enjoyed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, the narrative arc is an expected one. The idea of a power which bodes great promise, but later turns out to be a curse, is fairly common in stories about immortality, for instance. Hell, eternal youth is effectively a bastardised form of true immortality at any rate. So yeah, this feels like one of those stories. Can't say much about the journals that hasn't already been said, but they do their job well-enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can understand that. It’s hard to fill in all the gaps without things feeling too long or overwhelming, so I understand if you think I might’ve missed a few important details.

Thanks for the upvote though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a bit uncertain how I feel about this one - it's clearly written as a sucker punch to the feels, and I admit it nailed that shot, but…it's a little empty. Melancholy is a fair response, but it's not really aimed at the dead guy, and the question of what happened to kill him (despite his powers) feels tacked on, rather than an organic mystery.

It's still an upvote, but it's a weak one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
“Cursed By A Vengeful Care Bear” is probably a sentence I never thought I’d read but I’m glad I did.

Thanks for the upvote!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was completely unexpected. To take the compulsion effect depicted and make it the stuff of horror - it's original and well depicted. The poor man, just wanting to get out of the house and do something normal… it's like he was cursed by a vengeful Care Bear.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you Mendy! I understand why you wouldn’t like the journal entries, they aren’t exactly for everyone, but I’m glad you liked it otherwise!

If I ever rewrite this, I’ll consider the ending you suggested. It’s certainly an interesting take.

(Also I’m glad I finally made a skip you upvoted)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I do like the level of detail in this and the effort taken to explore the implications of his anomalous effect, there are a few things that feel off to me.

1. I don't see any reason why the guy's journal entries are logged here. This isn't really an awful thing, it's just that there doesn't really seem to be a reason to have the journals in the article except "plot". Which breaks immersion a bit for me. Just a bit.

2. The journals became a bit weak at the end. The angst at the end (i. e. "there was no love" etc) felt a bit forced. I personally feel that the article isn't at its full heart-wrench potential yet. Perhaps there could be further exploration into the theme of superficial affection versus real and sincere love. We could have the guy be all like "I can only trust my boyfriend now I'm not gonna leave home I'll stay with him forever" and then he bumps against the wall or something and his boyfriend, the only person who truly cared about him, go crazy due to anomalous effects. I think that'd make for a stronger, more cohesive ending, but this is just my 2 cents.

All in all, this is okay, and I liked it, so upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Who knows? It’s not something the Foundation are willing to test now, but maybe some day they’ll want to know.

Thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Interesting effect. Really emotional. I guess it couldn't cure death. Or could it, if they let people hug him enough?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That’s actually really sweet of you to say, I’m so glad that I evoked such strong emotions with this in you.

I’m seriously smiling so much, thank you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too often compulsion effects are a lazy substitute for good writing. It's nice to be reminded that they can be employed very well indeed in the hands of a skilled author. Very creepy existential horror delivered via excessive hugs.

I also want to applaud your journal entries here: I thought the pacing was excellent, telling the story evocatively and succinctly without feeling rushed or forced-marched. Very organic feel to it. You nearly had me in real tears with his line about not being able to escape by shooting up—that's some heartbreaking shit right there. Heartily upvoted!

(edited for typos)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I understand, compulsion effects have the potential to be a little touchy, but I'm glad that you liked it anyway!

Thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! I appreciate the kind words!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Although I'm not too much of a fan of compulsion effects, it actually felt pretty fitting here, and lead to some good character development. I also liked the detail of when SCP-3430 gets hurt, his anomalous effects kick in, but now that he's dead, the anomalous effects are activated regardless, which would make sense.

Overall, the article was a pretty good read.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The journal really makes this one for me. It hooked me in, started off as interesting, and ended off as heart-wrenching. Excellent work, +1

item.title: Re: XXXX
item.content:encoded: (Whoops)

item.title: XXXX
item.content:encoded: Othello, you forgot to replace the XXXX's.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to A Random Day, DarkStuff, The Great Hippo, Moklin, taylor_itkin, Shaggydredlocks, DrChandra, ArkadyRex, and anyone else who I might have forgotten for helping to crit this!

I hope you all enjoy!

Image Source
